channel.title: Chapter 4: Lexical Analysis (Scanner) on Docs
channel.description: Recent content in Chapter 4: Lexical Analysis (Scanner) on Docs

item.title: Chapter 4, Part 1 – What is a Lexical Analyzer?
item.description:
A Lexical Analyzer or Scanner is an algorithm that groups the characters of the source code to form Tokens, and returns the Internal Representation Number of these tokens — a kind of ID assigned to each token.

These tokens are divided into 3 kinds:

  1. Names — any name in a program, divided into 3 types:

    • Keywords/Reserved: words such as if, else, while. These cannot be used as variable names.
    • User Defined Names: names declared by the user.
    • Standard Identifiers: such as sin, cos, sqrt. Like reserved words, these cannot be used as variable names and are not included in production rules.

  2. Values — such as integers (1, 2, 3) or floating point (1.1, 2.34) etc.

item.title: Chapter 4, Part 2 – Regular Expressions
item.description:
Regular Languages are a class of language important for lexical analysis — we use them to define and generate tokens. This class is defined recursively.

The three types of tokens (names, values, special symbols) are all generated by regular expressions. The scanner matches input strings against these patterns to recognize valid tokens.

Defining a Language as a Set

Alphabet V — a set of symbols. For example: V = {a, b, ..., z}.

item.title: Chapter 4, Part 3 – Finite State Automata (FSA)
item.description:
The algorithm that recognizes strings of regular languages is called the Finite State Automata (FSA), also known as the Finite State Machine or Finite State System. It contains:

  1. A set of states.
  2. Transitions between states.
  3. An input string to be examined.

FSA Example

Consider this FSA for floating point numbers, with states Q = {S, A, B, G, H}:

  • S — Starting State
  • H — Final (Halt) State
  • Transitions: {+, −, d, .}

Tracking the input "-511.32" through the states:

item.title: Chapter 4, Part 4 – Creating a NDFSA from a Regular Expression
item.description:
FSM for Basic Tokens

Simple finite state machines for basic tokens:

Names — Letter(Letter|digit)* = L(L|d)*

S --L--> F
         |
      L,d (loop)

Integers — [+|−]digit(digit)* = [+|−]d+

S --+/--> A --d--> F
               |
             d (loop)

Both machines are deterministic: no λ-transitions, no state has multiple transitions on the same input.

Construction Methodology

To construct a NDFSA from a regular expression:

  1. Decompose the expression into primitive components:
